How to get the most out of this course

files.title: Data modeling for Power BI video course

aFEB67AF3-9D8B-1616-4647-978AC3603B6C.title: Practice by repeating demos
aFEB67AF3-9D8B-1616-4647-978AC3603B6C.text:
In this section, you can download the slides used in the video course as reference material.

You can also download the content used for the demos in the video course.

You should practice by redoing the demos on your computer:

  • Follow the instructions to install the prerequisites and the Contoso databases on SQL Server.
  • Download the ZIP file with the demo material and unzip the content in a local folder on your computer.
  • The reference of each demo is designed to match the structure to the folder where the demo material can be found. For example, M04.L03.D01 stands for Module 04, Lecture 03, Demo 01.
  • When there are different versions of the same file, the file with the "- begin" suffix is the initial state for the demo and the file with the "- end" suffix is the expected result if you apply the steps shown in the demo.

a757E4E02-236B-078E-854D-569C1577E140.title: Demos download
a757E4E02-236B-078E-854D-569C1577E140.text:
The ZIP file contains all the files used in the demos of the video course. Download the file and unzip the content in a local folder on your computer.

The BAK files are SQL Server backups that you can restore on SQL Server 2019. All the Contoso sample databases have the same structure, the only difference is their size. For example, Contoso 10K contains around 10,000 orders while Contoso 1M contains around 1 million orders. Most of the demos in the course use the Contoso 10K database, but different demos use different – larger – databases.

The Contoso 100M database is relatively large (more than 2GB) and it is used only in the demos for parallel processing of partitions. If your computer cannot manage such a large database, you can practice the demo using the smaller one (Contoso 10M).

If possible, we suggest restoring all the databases on your SQL Server 2019 instance. Follow the instructions at Installing the prerequisites / SSMS and the demo database

aE71BD41B-CD35-E8B4-E5AB-D6FE6C959D40.title: Slides of the video course
aE71BD41B-CD35-E8B4-E5AB-D6FE6C959D40.text:
You can download a PDF with all the slides used in the video course.

If you use the formats with 1, 2, and 6 slides per page the coverage of the page will be full – whereas with 3 slides per page you will have side space for your notes.

The grayscale format is optimized for black & white printing.

files.title: 3 slides per page with side space for notes - full color.pdf
3 slides per page with side space for notes - gray scale.pdf

a14B17950-02FF-7064-D08D-E32F8C38BB28.title: Introduction to Tabular models
a14B17950-02FF-7064-D08D-E32F8C38BB28.text: The Tabular model is the core of the Analysis Services architecture that runs Power BI, Power Pivot for Excel, SQL Server Analysis Services, and Azure Analysis Services. This section introduces the protocols used by the different versions of Analysis Services and the structure of the Tabular Object Model shared by many different services.

markers.title: Tools to create a Tabular model

aBAC5A099-6241-2C2E-0FD0-45C4F891C1F2.title: Installing the prerequisites
aBAC5A099-6241-2C2E-0FD0-45C4F891C1F2.text:
This module guides you in the installation of the software required to practice redoing the demos shown in the training.

You will see how to install:

  • SQL Server 2019 (you can install SQL Server 2022 instead of 2019, in that case, pay attention to additional notes added between video lectures).
  • SQL Server Analysis Services Tabular
  • SQL Server Management Studio
  • The Contoso demo databases
  • Visual Studio 2019 (you can install Visual Studio 2022 instead of 2019, the Analysis Services project extension is supported since March 2023).
  • Power BI Desktop
  • Excel
  • DAX Studio
  • Tabular Editor 2
  • Tabular Editor 3
  • Analyze in Excel for Power BI Desktop
  • ALM Toolkit

files.title: Analyze in Excel for Power BI Desktop

a51E38C9E-9A80-2EF6-47FC-086DDBA45C93.title: Important notes about SQL Server 2019 and 2022
a51E38C9E-9A80-2EF6-47FC-086DDBA45C93.text:
If you installed SQL Server 2022 instead of SQL Server 2019, there is an important difference in the next lecture: SSMS and the demo database / Demo database (setup).

The alias configuration in SQL Server 2022 is not possible if the computer does not have a previous installation of the SQL Server Native Client, which is made automatically in SQL Server 2019. If you want to configure database aliases on SQL Server 2022, please read the instructions described in this article: Creating database aliases on SQL Server 2022

a9B27EE30-6C45-66E7-26FA-1368D70EB396.title: Important notes about Visual Studio 2019 and 2022
a9B27EE30-6C45-66E7-26FA-1368D70EB396.text:
Since March 2023, the Microsoft Analysis Services project is available also for Visual Studio 2022, so you can use Visual Studio 2022 instead of Visual Studio 2019.

If you practiced the course before March 2023, you had to use Visual Studio 2019 because the Analysis Services projects extension was unavailable. This is no longer the case since March 2023.

However, if you still want to use Visual Studio 2019 to get the identical user interface as in the lectures, you can download Visual Studio 2019 at this link: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/releases/2019/system-requirements#download.

markers.title: Analyze in Excel for Power BI Desktop

a151C52DE-6EA8-2BB8-707D-577966EAC871.title: Guided tour on Tabular
a151C52DE-6EA8-2BB8-707D-577966EAC871.text:
Learn how to create a Tabular model from scratch using different tools: Power BI Desktop, Visual Studio with Analysis Services Projects, Tabular Editor 2, and Tabular Editor 3.

We strongly suggest you watch the demos with all the development tools, even if you already know some of the tools or if you plan to use only a few of them. We will not repeat the same concepts in all the tools. It is thus important to watch the complete list of lectures and demos in a sequential way the first time around, to make sure you understand all the different parts and tools involved in a complete Tabular model.

markers.title: Tools connected to a workspace database
Guided tour: how to watch the demos

a1BFD7EBA-6BF4-9A7D-3725-EF857DAC3C94.title: Understanding model types
a1BFD7EBA-6BF4-9A7D-3725-EF857DAC3C94.text:
There are different model types in Tabular (Import, DirectQuery, composite models) that can use different storage technologies: VertiPaq, DirectQuery for relational sources, and DirectQuery for Analysis Services.

This module explains the different architectures involved in each choice and clarifies the difference between a live connection to Analysis Services and a composite model with DirectQuery for Analysis Services.

a32D1B846-35D3-731F-E048-6A6CD568D9F0.title: Formula engine and storage engine
a32D1B846-35D3-731F-E048-6A6CD568D9F0.text: The DAX calculation engine uses two engines to execute a query: the formula engine and the storage engine. This module explains the different roles of these engines and how each query is executed in multiple steps using both engines.

a352D9B88-45FF-08AC-0E49-013A414480D9.title: Data sources for Tabular
a352D9B88-45FF-08AC-0E49-013A414480D9.text:
Every data model has one or more data sources that define how to retrieve data, by specifying the technology (legacy or structured), the driver, the authentication, and the transformations.

This module also explains the role of the data gateway in cloud solutions, how to work with large tables, and whether to map tables or views from a relational database.

files.title: Data Gateway (for Power BI and other Microsoft products)

a7E2226F1-F814-9BD5-8986-4CEF8CA769DF.title: Tabular presentation layer
a7E2226F1-F814-9BD5-8986-4CEF8CA769DF.text:
This module describes the attributes and the additional metadata you can use to enrich the Tabular semantic model. It explains the internal behavior of features that may be exposed under different names to end-users.

This is the core module of the Mastering Tabular training for model authors that want to know and manage all the features of the semantic model – including those that are not available in all the development environments. For example, Power BI Desktop can expose features that have a different name in the underlying Tabular model. Understanding these differences is important when the model is upgraded to Power BI service and/or managed by using Tabular Editor.

files.title: Data import best practices in Power BI

markers.title: The path of a column name
The path of a column name (demo)
Column names in Power BI Desktop
Column names in Power BI Desktop (demo)

aF15ED351-5E44-97A4-C361-42248E65FE58.title: Grouping columns - additional notes
aF15ED351-5E44-97A4-C361-42248E65FE58.text:
The "group by column" feature shown in the previous lecture is currently supported only by a few clients like Power BI.
MDX clients like Excel do not support such feature and the queries generated by those clients ignore the group by columns setting.
We will update this note when Microsoft will extend the support of "group by columns" to more clients.

a3A29A16B-8087-C01C-0E23-EBAAD4866426.title: Date table in Tabular and Power BI Desktop

a521FCE80-BC79-F754-0804-8C06A2F86955.title: Understanding relationships
a521FCE80-BC79-F754-0804-8C06A2F86955.text: Relationships have a central role in a Tabular model. The properties of each relationship affect the consistency of the model and the result produced by the reports. This module analyzes in detail all the properties available in each relationship and explains the side effects of filter propagation on security, data consistency, and ambiguity.

markers.title: Limited relationships: no expansion, no blank row
Things are more complex in a larger model
Never work with an ambiguous model

aCCF7B91E-1AEF-9E3E-A68D-1E5AE618F4A4.title: Handling security
aCCF7B91E-1AEF-9E3E-A68D-1E5AE618F4A4.text: A Tabular model has a role-based security system that can be used to set row-level and object-level security. This module explains how to implement static and dynamic row-level security and how you can combine object-level and row-level security in the same model.

markers.title: Testing security in Power BI Desktop
Testing security in Power BI Desktop (demo)

a7744B5BF-9801-465E-FDD9-82B305216241.title: Understanding data types
a7744B5BF-9801-465E-FDD9-82B305216241.text: Each column in a Tabular model has a specific data type: Integer, Decimal, Currency, DateTime, Boolean, String, Binary, or Variant. Each data type has different aliases in different products and a default mapping with common relational data sources. This module also explains possible conversion errors in calculations involving different numeric and date data types.

markers.title: Currency used only at the end

aB18B8C79-5473-D739-3953-CFA831407ACC.title: Understanding the VertiPaq engine
aB18B8C79-5473-D739-3953-CFA831407ACC.text: VertiPaq is the storage engine used to import data in memory. VertiPaq is the engine that provides the best query performance, but it requires more memory to keep data compressed in memory. Understanding the compression techniques used by VertiPaq can help you choose the right columns to import to optimize the memory used by a Tabular model.

aA2A7CB94-565B-1802-B39D-3EE882398C4E.title: VertiPaq segmentation update (Power BI Premium)
aA2A7CB94-565B-1802-B39D-3EE882398C4E.text:
Since October 2021, Microsoft changed the segment size used in large models deployed on a Power BI Premium capacity.

While the default segment size for Power BI is always 1 million rows, if a dataset uses the large dataset storage format, its segment size is raised to 8 million rows, like the default segment size in Analysis Services.

You can find more information about large dataset storage format in Power BI Premium in the Microsoft documentation:
Large datasets in Power BI Premium - Power BI | Microsoft Docs

You can see more details about the importance of a larger segment size for big tables (100M rows or more) in this SQLBI video:
Explaining segment size in Power BI Premium – Unplugged #29 - SQLBI

markers.title: What to store in the model?

a2A786203-6E2E-BEE4-AB4F-9DA446B9A021.title: Understanding DirectQuery
a2A786203-6E2E-BEE4-AB4F-9DA446B9A021.text: DirectQuery is the storage engine used when data is retrieved from the data source at query time. Technically, DirectQuery is a query technology and not a real storage engine: When you use DirectQuery, the data source becomes the storage engine used to execute MDX and DAX queries. This module explains the main differences between VertiPaq and DirectQuery – it shows you how to create a Tabular model in DirectQuery and what to expect in terms of data source workload at query time.

files.title: DirectQuery supported structured data sources (Power Query)
How to set Max Intermediate Row Set Count property in Power BI Premium

a3ABD0DAF-AD8C-2FC0-6785-E488858D555A.title: Understanding DirectQuery over Analysis Services
a3ABD0DAF-AD8C-2FC0-6785-E488858D555A.text: DirectQuery over Analysis Services is a special kind of DirectQuery where the data source is not just a set of tables, but rather another semantic model hosted by an Analysis Services instance. Because a Power BI dataset is an Analysis Services database, the same technology can be used to connect external Power BI datasets or Azure Analysis Services databases, extending them with additional tables and relationships. These models are also called "composite models" because they mix different storage engine technologies and different semantic models within one same model.

markers.title: The local model extends the remote model
Import Budget Excel workbook in Contoso (demo)

a761239D3-199B-AB93-321F-2DEA63AAA58C.title: Query resolution in DirectQuery over AS

markers.title: Creating calculated columns on composite models (demo)

aCE3E351A-F751-6B59-DEF1-D1A9F69DA341.title: Aggregations
aCE3E351A-F751-6B59-DEF1-D1A9F69DA341.text: You can improve the performance of the DirectQuery storage engine by creating aggregations, which can be stored in the original data source or imported in VertiPaq tables. This module explains all the options available for you to take advantage of aggregations for DirectQuery in a Tabular model.

markers.title: Aggregations are alternate of model columns
Aggregations at the table key level (demo)
A pure DirectQuery query would be better

a5590EB41-3D58-E1D6-EFCD-C4F486DE8F84.title: Push Datasets
a5590EB41-3D58-E1D6-EFCD-C4F486DE8F84.text:
The push dataset in the Power BI service is a special kind of Tabular model that receives data from an application instead of pulling data from a data source.

This module explains how to create hybrid real-time solutions using both regular datasets and push datasets to serve all different user requirements for real-time and historical analytics.

files.title: Implementing real-time updates in Power BI using push datasets instead of DirectQuery
Time Intelligence in Power BI Desktop: Adding a Dummy Fact table
Creating a service principal account for Power BI API

aA58FD419-3CFE-6087-A0FA-DAE5697288E4.title: Implementing real-time solutions with push datasets

markers.title: Creating a push dataset starting from a Power BI Dataset (demo)

a4E944E57-E8BC-BF5B-0ADB-2AA07625D073.title: Hardware considerations
a4E944E57-E8BC-BF5B-0ADB-2AA07625D073.text: This module contains important information to make the right hardware choices for on-premises solutions, and the best choice of service tier for cloud solutions. It also includes tips for instances running on virtual machines.

files.title: Optimize hardware settings or Analysis Services Tabular

aEBD80FA6-5398-53A7-7280-7585DD58B4CE.title: Architectural choices
aEBD80FA6-5398-53A7-7280-7585DD58B4CE.text:
This module is a recap of the architectural choices that you should consider when creating a solution based on a Tabular model.

All the concepts you have seen in previous modules raise questions you should answer prior to implementing an enterprise-level solution. The answers depend on your specific requirements: this module should guide you in making the right decisions.

files.title: Understand star schema and the importance for Power BI

markers.title: AS on prem or in the cloud?
Star schema is the way to go
Composite models with DirectQuery over SQL
How frequently do you need to refresh?
What are your report’s time requirements?

a3D8A95AE-29B8-E2A9-FA60-92E9AF6CBB3D.title: Managing Tabular
a3D8A95AE-29B8-E2A9-FA60-92E9AF6CBB3D.text: This short module introduces the second part of the training dedicated to deploying, monitoring, and maintaining a Tabular model in production.

markers.title: The model is ready... what's next?

a172BAA5E-DC0F-0138-E841-47FDFEF41016.title: Interfacing with Tabular
a172BAA5E-DC0F-0138-E841-47FDFEF41016.text: You can use several protocols, APIs, and libraries to communicate with a Tabular model. This module guides you through all the options available. This module is important to implement automatic and/or scheduled operations.

files.title: Azure Analysis Services – Asynchronous refresh with the REST API
Power BI Embedded ARM REST API

markers.title: Example: refresh a table using TOM
Example: refresh a table using TMSL
Example: refresh a table using AAS REST API
Try the Power BI REST API (demo)

aB3526780-CF31-48AC-F673-B02C68326B90.title: Deploying Tabular models
aB3526780-CF31-48AC-F673-B02C68326B90.text:
Deploying a model in production requires proper planning to reduce or remove any downtime, and to maximize the use of the available server resources. 

This module clarifies the impact of deploying different types of changes on a model in production. It also clarifies how to use different tools to control the side effects and minimize their impact on service availability.

files.title: Analysis Services Deploymnet Wizard (installed with SQL Server Management Studio)
CID/CD with AzureDevOps, Git, and Tabular Editor You're Deploying it Wrong! - AS Edition
Enable XMLA read-write on Power BI Premium to deploy Tabular models

markers.title: Deploying a solution from Visual Studio (demo)

a27467846-E9AC-720B-B3F4-7943F111D224.title: Processing and partitioning Tabular models
a27467846-E9AC-720B-B3F4-7943F111D224.text: A large table can be split into partitions to reduce processing time. This module describes how to define a partitioning strategy to minimize refresh time, and how to reduce the processing time through parallelism.

files.title: Azure Logic Apps: internally call REST API

markers.title: Improve processing time by using partitions

a5A2B9798-5AE6-68C5-B3F3-D9B4653602D5.title: Monitoring Tabular
a5A2B9798-5AE6-68C5-B3F3-D9B4653602D5.text:
There are different tools available to monitor the behavior of a Tabular model: CPU consumption, memory consumption, active sessions, and events describing the operations executed by the engine.

This module is a quick guide to the tools available to you, and to the more important metrics to monitor and control in an Analysis Services instance.

files.title: Optimizing memory settings in Analysis Services
Visualizing Azure Analysis Services processing tasks with the Job Graph events sample
Diagnostic Logs of Azure Analysis Services

markers.title: Expensive request going out of memory
Memory settings for Power BI service
Monitoring refresh operation using SQL Server Profiler (demo)
Monitoring a query using SQL Server Profiler and DAX Studio (demo)

a0C0C78DD-5713-8903-0B99-969DA3744974.title: Backup and restore strategies
a0C0C78DD-5713-8903-0B99-969DA3744974.text: This module describes the differences between backup/restore and attach/detach of a database, and how to use these features in different products.

files.title: Azure Analysis Services backup (Backup Storage Settings)

aDDD5AA0E-C251-5118-2F93-6B91A87A552C.title: Executing attach, detach, backup, and restore

markers.title: Backup and restore on Power BI Premium
Backup and restore on Power BI Premium (demo)

a1BC793B7-0A9C-2406-166F-22D2BC623F51.title: Scaling out
a1BC793B7-0A9C-2406-166F-22D2BC623F51.text: Analysis Services can be configured in a scale-out architecture, with implementation differences between an on-premises solution and Azure Analysis Services.

markers.title: Scaling out with on-premises Analysis Services
Scaling out with on-premises Analysis Services (demo)
Scaling out with Azure Analysis Services
Scaling out with Azure Analysis Services (demo)
Scaling out with Power BI service
